summary: The authors achieved 2nd place by implementing a two-stage pipeline designed for simplicity and robustness across highly diverse DICOM data. Stage 1 uses a 2D nnU-Net to extract tri-axial ROIs, while Stage 2 employs a 3D multi-task nnU-Net for joint vessel/aneurysm segmentation and classification. Key innovations include cross-attention pooling, modality classification heads, targeted oversampling for rare classes, and heavy test-time augmentation with label swapping. They also leveraged external TopCoW and TopBrain datasets, manually corrected systematic annotation errors, and identified a public-private distribution shift that guided their final inference strategy.
extensive_summary:
## Pipeline Overview
The solution adopts a two-stage approach optimized for speed and generality. Stage 1 performs fast 2D tri-axial ROI extraction using a 2D nnU-Net segmentation model to crop binary vascular regions. Stage 2 unifies all spacing and modalities by resizing inputs to 224x224x224 and runs a 3D multi-task nnU-Net that jointly performs vessel and aneurysm segmentation alongside classification.

## Key Technical Enhancements
The 3D multi-task model incorporates several architectural and training modifications. Cross-attention pooling replaced average pooling to accelerate convergence, and a modality 4-class head was added to stabilize training. To address class imbalance, rare classes were oversampled and assigned higher cross-entropy weights. Data augmentation heavily relied on left-right flips with corresponding label and mask swaps, effectively doubling the dataset size. Inference was optimized for speed by forwarding only the encoder and classification head.

## Data Handling and External Resources
The team faced significant preprocessing challenges due to multi-frame DICOMs, deleted test fields, and varying spacings. They switched to pydicom, mapped spacing by slice count, and trained a T2-specific orientation classifier. They manually corrected annotation errors in the provided CSV (e.g., left/right side mix-ups) and iteratively refined aneurysm masks using center-point distance heatmaps. External data from TopCoW and TopBrain challenges was incorporated to improve vessel segmentation masks, though the LargeIA dataset was excluded.

## Training and Inference Strategy
Training followed nnU-Net defaults from scratch, using cross-entropy loss with heatmap weighting for aneurysm centers. The final submission utilized a conservative two-fold ensemble run on 2x T4 GPUs, taking ~9 hours. Heavy TTA (8x flips with label swapping) was applied during inference. The authors noted that removing a 0.5 prediction fallback revealed a consistent 3–4% gap between public and private leaderboards, indicating a distribution shift in abnormal cases.
competition_flow: Raw multi-frame DICOMs are preprocessed and resized to 224x224x224, passed through a 2D nnU-Net for tri-axial ROI extraction, then fed into a 3D multi-task nnU-Net for joint vessel/aneurysm segmentation and classification, with heavy TTA and a two-fold ensemble producing the final submission.
data_reading: Multi-frame DICOM files parsed via pydicom (after dicom2nifti failed on deleted test fields), with spacing mapped by slice count and T2-specific orientation classification applied.
data_processing: Spacing and orientation corrected via slice-count mapping and a T2 classifier
External TopCoW and TopBrain annotations merged to refine vessel masks
Manual correction of left/right and anatomical label mix-ups
Left-right flips with label/mask swapping for augmentation
Rare class oversampling with higher cross-entropy weights
Heavy TTA (8x flips with label swapping)
loss_functions: Cross-entropy loss with heatmap weighting for aneurysm centers
cv_strategy: Multi-fold validation (specifically a two-fold ensemble mentioned in ablation), validated locally on all training cases.
ensembling: The final submission uses a conservative two-fold model ensemble combined with heavy test-time augmentation (8x left-right flips with label swapping) to maximize robustness and inference speed.
insights: Stage 1 2D tri-axial ROI extraction significantly improved inference speed.
Incorporating vessel segmentation clearly enhanced both aneurysm segmentation and classification performance.
Noise-handling techniques proved more effective on the public dataset than the private one.
Removing the 0.5 prediction fallback exposed a consistent 3–4% public-private leaderboard gap, indicating a distribution shift in abnormal cases.
critical_findings: The provided RSNA aneurysm annotations contained systematic left/right and anatomical position mix-ups that required manual correction to prevent performance degradation.
Relying on a 0.5 prediction fallback masked a consistent 3–4% performance gap between public and private leaderboards, revealing a distribution shift in abnormal cases.
Increasing public leaderboard scores consistently translated to private leaderboard improvements, validating the chosen augmentation and annotation refinement strategies.
what_did_not_work: Using dicom2nifti failed on test sets with deleted fields, necessitating a switch to pydicom.
Implementing try-except fallbacks to 0.5 predictions reduced robustness and was removed from the final inference pipeline.

papers.title: nnU-Net: a self-configuring method for deep learning-based biomedical image segmentation
Benchmarking the CoW with the TopCoW Challenge: Topology-Aware Anatomical Segmentation of the Circle of Willis for CTA and MRA
